content.heading: Scope — what can each part of your code see?

body.text: Scope determines which variables a piece of code can access. Variables declared outside any function are global — visible everywhere. Variables declared inside a function are local — only visible inside that function.

body.desc: Global scope — any function can read or change this
Local scope — only visible inside the function it was declared in

lines: let secret = 42;    // local — only visible inside increment()
count = count + 1;  // works — count is global
console.log(secret);    // ReferenceError — secret does not exist here

body.label: Do not explain this first
body.text: Have students predict whether the last line will work, then run it and read the error. A ReferenceError is more memorable than a definition of scope.

exercises.title: Break scope on purpose
tasks: The code below throws a ReferenceError. Read it and predict which line causes the error before running.
Run it and confirm. Read the error message in the console carefully.
Fix it by moving the offending line to where secret is actually visible.
Now try the reverse: declare a variable globally and confirm a function can read it.
exercises.hint: A variable declared with let inside a function only exists inside that function's curly braces.

checks.label: logs something from inside the function
a ReferenceError appears in the output

exercises.title: Global vs local scope
tasks: Declare a variable called total set to 0 outside any function.
Write a function called addFive that adds 5 to total each time it is called.
Call addFive three times and log total after each call.
Now declare a variable called localOnly inside addFive. Try to log it outside the function. Read the error.
exercises.hint: total is global so addFive can read and change it. localOnly is local so nothing outside addFive can see it.
